The Terrifying A.I. Scam That Uses Your Loved One\xe2\x80\x99s Voice

The New Yorker · by Charles Bethea · March 7, 2024
Search
Annals of Artificial Intelligence
A Brooklyn couple got a call from relatives who were being held ransom. Their voices\xe2\x80\x94like many others these days\xe2\x80\x94had been cloned.
March 7, 2024

On a recent night, a woman named Robin was asleep next to her husband, Steve, in their Brooklyn home, when her phone buzzed on the bedside table. Robin is in her mid-thirties with long, dirty-blond hair. She works as an interior designer, specializing in luxury homes. The couple had gone out to a natural-wine bar in Cobble Hill that evening, and had come home a few hours earlier and gone to bed. Their two young children were asleep in bedrooms down the hall. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m always, like, kind of one ear awake,\xe2\x80\x9d Robin told me, recently. When her phone rang, she opened her eyes and looked at the caller I.D. It was her mother-in-law, Mona, who never called after midnight. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m, like, maybe it\xe2\x80\x99s a butt-dial,\xe2\x80\x9d Robin said. \xe2\x80\x9cSo I ignore it, and I try to roll over and go back to bed. But then I see it pop up again.\xe2\x80\x9d

She picked up the phone, and, on the other end, she heard Mona\xe2\x80\x99s voice wailing and repeating the words \xe2\x80\x9cI can\xe2\x80\x99t do it, I can\xe2\x80\x99t do it.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cI thought she was trying to tell me that some horrible tragic thing had happened,\xe2\x80\x9d Robin told me. Mona and her husband, Bob, are in their seventies. She\xe2\x80\x99s a retired party planner, and he\xe2\x80\x99s a dentist. They spend the warm months in Bethesda, Maryland, and winters in Boca Raton, where they play pickleball and canasta. Robin\xe2\x80\x99s first thought was that there had been an accident. Robin\xe2\x80\x99s parents also winter in Florida, and she pictured the four of them in a car wreck. \xe2\x80\x9cYour brain does weird things in the middle of the night,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. Robin then heard what sounded like Bob\xe2\x80\x99s voice on the phone. (The family members requested that their names be changed to protect their privacy.) \xe2\x80\x9cMona, pass me the phone,\xe2\x80\x9d Bob\xe2\x80\x99s voice said, then, \xe2\x80\x9cGet Steve. Get Steve.\xe2\x80\x9d Robin took this\xe2\x80\x94that they didn\xe2\x80\x99t want to tell her while she was alone\xe2\x80\x94as another sign of their seriousness. She shook Steve awake. \xe2\x80\x9cI think it\xe2\x80\x99s your mom,\xe2\x80\x9d she told him. \xe2\x80\x9cI think she\xe2\x80\x99s telling me something terrible happened.\xe2\x80\x9d

Steve, who has close-cropped hair and an athletic build, works in law enforcement. When he opened his eyes, he found Robin in a state of panic. \xe2\x80\x9cShe was screaming,\xe2\x80\x9d he recalled. \xe2\x80\x9cI thought her whole family was dead.\xe2\x80\x9d When he took the phone, he heard a relaxed male voice\xe2\x80\x94possibly Southern\xe2\x80\x94on the other end of the line. \xe2\x80\x9cYou\xe2\x80\x99re not gonna call the police,\xe2\x80\x9d the man said. \xe2\x80\x9cYou\xe2\x80\x99re not gonna tell anybody. I\xe2\x80\x99ve got a gun to your mom\xe2\x80\x99s head, and I\xe2\x80\x99m gonna blow her brains out if you don\xe2\x80\x99t do exactly what I say.\xe2\x80\x9d

Steve used his own phone to call a colleague with experience in hostage negotiations. The colleague was muted, so that he could hear the call but wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t be heard. \xe2\x80\x9cYou hear this???\xe2\x80\x9d Steve texted him. \xe2\x80\x9cWhat should I do?\xe2\x80\x9d The colleague wrote back, \xe2\x80\x9cTaking notes. Keep talking.\xe2\x80\x9d The idea, Steve said, was to continue the conversation, delaying violence and trying to learn any useful information.

\xe2\x80\x9cI want to hear her voice,\xe2\x80\x9d Steve said to the man on the phone.

The man refused. \xe2\x80\x9cIf you ask me that again, I\xe2\x80\x99m gonna kill her,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cAre you fucking crazy?\xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x9cO.K.,\xe2\x80\x9d Steve said. \xe2\x80\x9cWhat do you want?\xe2\x80\x9d

The man demanded money for travel; he wanted five hundred dollars, sent through Venmo. \xe2\x80\x9cIt was such an insanely small amount of money for a human being,\xe2\x80\x9d Steve recalled. \xe2\x80\x9cBut also: I\xe2\x80\x99m obviously gonna pay this.\xe2\x80\x9d Robin, listening in, reasoned that someone had broken into Steve\xe2\x80\x99s parents\xe2\x80\x99 home to hold them up for a little cash. On the phone, the man gave Steve a Venmo account to send the money to. It didn\xe2\x80\x99t work, so he tried a few more, and eventually found one that did. The app asked what the transaction was for.

\xe2\x80\x9cPut in a pizza emoji,\xe2\x80\x9d the man said.

After Steve sent the five hundred dollars, the man patched in a female voice\xe2\x80\x94a girlfriend, it seemed\xe2\x80\x94who said that the money had come through, but that it wasn\xe2\x80\x99t enough. Steve asked if his mother would be released, and the man got upset that he was bringing this up with the woman listening. \xe2\x80\x9cWhoa, whoa, whoa,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cBaby, I\xe2\x80\x99ll call you later.\xe2\x80\x9d The implication, to Steve, was that the woman didn\xe2\x80\x99t know about the hostage situation. \xe2\x80\x9cThat made it even more real,\xe2\x80\x9d Steve told me. The man then asked for an additional two hundred and fifty dollars to get a ticket for his girlfriend. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve gotta get my baby mama down here to me,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. Steve sent the additional sum, and, when it processed, the man hung up.

By this time, about twenty-five minutes had elapsed. Robin cried and Steve spoke to his colleague. \xe2\x80\x9cYou guys did great,\xe2\x80\x9d the colleague said. He told them to call Bob, since Mona\xe2\x80\x99s phone was clearly compromised, to make sure that he and Mona were now safe. After a few tries, Bob picked up the phone and handed it to Mona. \xe2\x80\x9cAre you at home?\xe2\x80\x9d Steve and Robin asked her. \xe2\x80\x9cAre you O.K.?\xe2\x80\x9d

Mona sounded fine, but she was unsure of what they were talking about. \xe2\x80\x9cYeah, I\xe2\x80\x99m in bed,\xe2\x80\x9d she replied. \xe2\x80\x9cWhy?\xe2\x80\x9d

Artificial intelligence is revolutionizing seemingly every aspect of our lives: medical diagnosis, weather forecasting, space exploration, and even mundane tasks like writing e-mails and searching the Internet. But with increased efficiencies and computational accuracy has come a Pandora\xe2\x80\x99s box of trouble. Deepfake video content is proliferating across the Internet. The month after Russia invaded Ukraine, a video surfaced on social media in which Ukraine\xe2\x80\x99s President, Volodymyr Zelensky, appeared to tell his troops to surrender. (He had not done so.) In early February of this year, Hong Kong police announced that a finance worker had been tricked into paying out twenty-five million dollars after taking part in a video conference with who he thought were members of his firm\xe2\x80\x99s senior staff. (They were not.) Thanks to large language models like ChatGPT, phishing e-mails have grown increasingly sophisticated, too. Steve and Robin, meanwhile, fell victim to another new scam, which uses A.I. to replicate a loved one\xe2\x80\x99s voice. \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99ve now passed through the uncanny valley,\xe2\x80\x9d Hany Farid, who studies generative A.I. and manipulated media at the University of California, Berkeley, told me. \xe2\x80\x9cI can now clone the voice of just about anybody and get them to say just about anything. And what you think would happen is exactly what\xe2\x80\x99s happening.\xe2\x80\x9d

Robots aping human voices are not new, of course. In 1984, an Apple computer became one of the first that could read a text file in a tinny robotic voice of its own. \xe2\x80\x9cHello, I\xe2\x80\x99m Macintosh,\xe2\x80\x9d a squat machine announced to a live audience, at an unveiling with Steve Jobs. \xe2\x80\x9cIt sure is great to get out of that bag.\xe2\x80\x9d The computer took potshots at Apple\xe2\x80\x99s main competitor at the time, saying, \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99d like to share with you a maxim I thought of the first time I met an I.B.M. mainframe: never trust a computer you can\xe2\x80\x99t lift.\xe2\x80\x9d In 2011, Apple released Siri; inspired by \xe2\x80\x9cStar Trek\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x99s talking computers, the program could interpret precise commands\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x9cPlay Steely Dan,\xe2\x80\x9d say, or, \xe2\x80\x9cCall Mom\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\x94and respond with a limited vocabulary. Three years later, Amazon released Alexa. Synthesized voices were cohabiting with us.

Still, until a few years ago, advances in synthetic voices had plateaued. They weren\xe2\x80\x99t entirely convincing. \xe2\x80\x9cIf I\xe2\x80\x99m trying to create a better version of Siri or G.P.S., what I care about is naturalness,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid explained. \xe2\x80\x9cDoes this sound like a human being and not like this creepy half-human, half-robot thing?\xe2\x80\x9d Replicating a specific voice is even harder. \xe2\x80\x9cNot only do I have to sound human,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid went on. \xe2\x80\x9cI have to sound like you.\xe2\x80\x9d In recent years, though, the problem began to benefit from more money, more data\xe2\x80\x94importantly, troves of voice recordings online\xe2\x80\x94and breakthroughs in the underlying software used for generating speech. In 2019, this bore fruit: a Toronto-based A.I. company called Dessa cloned the podcaster Joe Rogan\xe2\x80\x99s voice. (Rogan responded with \xe2\x80\x9cawe\xe2\x80\x9d and acceptance on Instagram, at the time, adding, \xe2\x80\x9cThe future is gonna be really fucking weird, kids.\xe2\x80\x9d) But Dessa needed a lot of money and hundreds of hours of Rogan\xe2\x80\x99s very available voice to make their product. Their success was a one-off.

In 2022, though, a New York-based company called ElevenLabs unveiled a service that produced impressive clones of virtually any voice quickly; breathing sounds had been incorporated, and more than two dozen languages could be cloned. ElevenLabs\xe2\x80\x99s technology is now widely available. \xe2\x80\x9cYou can just navigate to an app, pay five dollars a month, feed it forty-five seconds of someone\xe2\x80\x99s voice, and then clone that voice,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid told me. The company is now valued at more than a billion dollars, and the rest of Big Tech is chasing closely behind. The designers of Microsoft\xe2\x80\x99s Vall-E cloning program, which d\xc3\xa9buted last year, used sixty thousand hours of English-language audiobook narration from more than seven thousand speakers. Vall-E, which is not available to the public, can reportedly replicate the voice and \xe2\x80\x9cacoustic environment\xe2\x80\x9d of a speaker with just a three-second sample.

Voice-cloning technology has undoubtedly improved some lives. The Voice Keeper is among a handful of companies that are now \xe2\x80\x9cbanking\xe2\x80\x9d the voices of those suffering from voice-depriving diseases like A.L.S., Parkinson\xe2\x80\x99s, and throat cancer, so that, later, they can continue speaking with their own voice through text-to-speech software. A South Korean company recently launched what it describes as the first \xe2\x80\x9cAI memorial service,\xe2\x80\x9d which allows people to \xe2\x80\x9clive in the cloud\xe2\x80\x9d after their deaths and \xe2\x80\x9cspeak\xe2\x80\x9d to future generations. The company suggests that this can \xe2\x80\x9calleviate the pain of the death of your loved ones.\xe2\x80\x9d The technology has other legal, if less altruistic, applications. Celebrities can use voice-cloning programs to \xe2\x80\x9cloan\xe2\x80\x9d their voices to record advertisements and other content: the College Football Hall of Famer Keith Byars, for example, recently let a chicken chain in Ohio use a clone of his voice to take orders. The film industry has also benefitted. Actors in films can now \xe2\x80\x9cspeak\xe2\x80\x9d other languages\xe2\x80\x94English, say, when a foreign movie is released in the U.S. \xe2\x80\x9cThat means no more subtitles, and no more dubbing,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid said. \xe2\x80\x9cEverybody can speak whatever language you want.\xe2\x80\x9d Multiple publications, including The New Yorker, use ElevenLabs to offer audio narrations of stories. Last year, New York\xe2\x80\x99s mayor, Eric Adams, sent out A.I.-enabled robocalls in Mandarin and Yiddish\xe2\x80\x94languages he does not speak. (Privacy advocates called this a \xe2\x80\x9ccreepy vanity project.\xe2\x80\x9d)

But, more often, the technology seems to be used for nefarious purposes, like fraud. This has become easier now that TikTok, YouTube, and Instagram store endless videos of regular people talking. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s simple,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid explained. \xe2\x80\x9cYou take thirty or sixty seconds of a kid\xe2\x80\x99s voice and log in to ElevenLabs, and pretty soon Grandma\xe2\x80\x99s getting a call in Grandson\xe2\x80\x99s voice saying, \xe2\x80\x98Grandma, I\xe2\x80\x99m in trouble, I\xe2\x80\x99ve been in an accident.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d A financial request is almost always the end game. Farid went on, \xe2\x80\x9cAnd here\xe2\x80\x99s the thing: the bad guy can fail ninety-nine per cent of the time, and they will still become very, very rich. It\xe2\x80\x99s a numbers game.\xe2\x80\x9d The prevalence of these illegal efforts is difficult to measure, but, anecdotally, they\xe2\x80\x99ve been on the rise for a few years. In 2020, a corporate attorney in Philadelphia took a call from what he thought was his son, who said he had been injured in a car wreck involving a pregnant woman and needed nine thousand dollars to post bail. (He found out it was a scam when his daughter-in-law called his son\xe2\x80\x99s office, where he was safely at work.) In January, voters in New Hampshire received a robocall call from Joe Biden\xe2\x80\x99s voice telling them not to vote in the primary. (The man who admitted to generating the call said that he had used ElevenLabs software.) \xe2\x80\x9cI didn\xe2\x80\x99t think about it at the time that it wasn\xe2\x80\x99t his real voice,\xe2\x80\x9d an elderly Democrat in New Hampshire told the Associated Press. \xe2\x80\x9cThat\xe2\x80\x99s how convincing it was.\xe2\x80\x9d

Predictably, technology has outstripped regulation. Current copyright laws don\xe2\x80\x99t protect a person\xe2\x80\x99s voice. \xe2\x80\x9cA key question is whether authentication tools can keep up with advances in deepfake synthesis,\xe2\x80\x9d Senator Jon Ossoff, of Georgia, who chaired a Senate Judiciary Committee hearing on the matter last year, told me. \xe2\x80\x9cCan we get good enough fast enough at discerning real from fake, or will we lose the ability to verify the authenticity of voices, images, video, and other media?\xe2\x80\x9d He described the matter as an \xe2\x80\x9curgent\xe2\x80\x9d one for lawmakers. In January, a bipartisan group introduced the QUIET Act, which would increase penalties for those who use A.I. to impersonate people. In Arizona, a state senator introduced a bill that would designate A.I. as a weapon when used in conjunction with a crime, also allowing lengthier sentences.

The Federal Trade Commission, which investigates consumer fraud, reported that Americans lost more than two million dollars to impostor scams of various kinds in 2022. Last year, the F.T.C. put out a voice-cloning advisory, noting, \xe2\x80\x9cIf the caller says to wire money, send cryptocurrency, or buy gift cards and give them the card numbers and PINs, those could be signs of a scam.\xe2\x80\x9d But it, too, has not yet created any guidelines for the use of voice-cloning technology. Even if laws are enacted, policing them will be exceedingly difficult. Scammers can use encrypted apps to execute their schemes, and calls are completed in minutes. \xe2\x80\x9cBy the time you get there, the scam is over, and everybody\xe2\x80\x99s moved on,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid said.

A decade ago, the F.T.C. sponsored a competition to counter the rise of robocalls, and one of its winners went on to create Nomorobo, a call-blocking service that has helped to reduce\xe2\x80\x94but not eliminate\xe2\x80\x94the phenomenon. Late last year, the commission offered a twenty-five-thousand-dollar prize for the development of new ways to protect consumers from voice cloning. It received around seventy-five submissions, which focus on prevention, authentication, and real-time detection. Some of the submissions use artificial intelligence, while others rely on metadata or watermarking. (Judging will be completed by April.) Will Maxson, who is managing the F.T.C.\xe2\x80\x99s challenge, told me, \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99re hoping we\xe2\x80\x99ll spur some innovators to come up with products and services that will help reduce this new threat.\xe2\x80\x9d But it\xe2\x80\x99s not at all clear how effective they will be. \xe2\x80\x9cThere are no silver bullets,\xe2\x80\x9d he acknowledged.

A few months ago, Farid, the Berkeley professor, participated in a Zoom call with Barack Obama. The former President was interested, he said, in learning about generative A.I. During the Zoom call, Farid found himself in an increasingly familiar online state of mind: doubt. \xe2\x80\x9cPeople have spent so much time trying to make deepfakes with Obama that I spent, like, the first ten minutes being, like, I don\xe2\x80\x99t know, man, I don\xe2\x80\x99t think this is him,\xe2\x80\x9d he said, laughing. In the end, he determined that it was the real Obama. Still, the experience was unnerving. \xe2\x80\x9cShit\xe2\x80\x99s getting weird,\xe2\x80\x9d he said.

One Friday last January, Jennifer DeStefano, who lives in Scottsdale, Arizona, got a call while walking into a dance studio where the younger of her two teen-age daughters, Aubrey, had just wrapped up a rehearsal. The caller I.D. read \xe2\x80\x9cunknown,\xe2\x80\x9d so DeStefano ignored it at first. Then she reconsidered: Brianna, her older daughter, was on a ski trip up north, and, DeStefano thought, maybe something had happened. She took the call on speaker phone. \xe2\x80\x9cMom, I messed up!\xe2\x80\x9d Briana\xe2\x80\x99s voice said, sobbing in her uniquely controlled way. A man with a Spanish accent could be heard telling her, \xe2\x80\x9cLay down and put your head back.\xe2\x80\x9d Then Briana said, \xe2\x80\x9cMom, these bad men have me. Help me, help me, help me.\xe2\x80\x9d One of the men took the phone, as Briana sobbed and pleaded in the background. \xe2\x80\x9cI have your daughter,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cIf you seek any help from anyone, I\xe2\x80\x99ll pump her stomach so full of drugs.\xe2\x80\x9d He\xe2\x80\x99d have his way with her, he continued, and then he\xe2\x80\x99d leave her for dead.

DeStefano ran into the dance studio and screamed for help. Three other mothers responded: one called 911, one called DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s husband, and one sat with DeStefano while she talked on the phone. First, the man demanded a million dollars, but DeStefano said that wasn\xe2\x80\x99t possible, so he lowered the sum to fifty thousand. As they discussed how to get the money to him, the mother who\xe2\x80\x99d called 911 came back inside and said that she\xe2\x80\x99d learned that the call might be a scam. DeStefano, who considers herself \xe2\x80\x9cpretty savvy,\xe2\x80\x9d was unconvinced. \xe2\x80\x9cI talked to her,\xe2\x80\x9d DeStefano replied. She continued speaking to the man, who decided that he wanted to arrange a physical pickup of the money: a white van would meet DeStefano somewhere, and someone would put a bag over her head, and bring her to him. She recalled, \xe2\x80\x9cHe said I had better have all the cash, or else we were both dead.\xe2\x80\x9d

Soon, though, the second mother hurried over. She had located DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s husband, who confirmed that he was with Briana. DeStefano eventually got ahold of her older daughter. \xe2\x80\x9cI have no idea what\xe2\x80\x99s going on, or what you\xe2\x80\x99re talking about,\xe2\x80\x9d Briana told her. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m with Dad.\xe2\x80\x9d Eventually, DeStefano returned to her phone call. \xe2\x80\x9cI called the guys out for being the lowest of the low,\xe2\x80\x9d DeStefano said. \xe2\x80\x9cI used vulgar words. Then I just hung up.\xe2\x80\x9d

DeStefano went public with her experience, eventually testifying about it before the Senate Judiciary Committee. Other victims reached out. Another mother at the dance studio had a cousin who\xe2\x80\x99d been scammed just two weeks earlier. \xe2\x80\x9cThe call came in from her daughter\xe2\x80\x99s phone, and she actually sent fifteen hundred dollars,\xe2\x80\x9d DeStefano said. She told me that a friend had received a call from what sounded like her nine-year-old son: \xe2\x80\x9cHe\xe2\x80\x99d been kidnapped, he said. But she\xe2\x80\x99d just tucked him in bed after reading a story, so she knew it wasn\xe2\x80\x99t true.\xe2\x80\x9d

RaeLee Jorgensen, a thirty-four-year-old teacher\xe2\x80\x99s aide, contacted DeStefano. Last April, while waiting for her two youngest children to get out of school, she received a phone call from her oldest son\xe2\x80\x99s number. \xe2\x80\x9cHey, Mom,\xe2\x80\x9d her fourteen-year-old son\xe2\x80\x99s voice said. \xe2\x80\x9cThis is Tate.\xe2\x80\x9d He was using his family nickname. \xe2\x80\x9cAnd it was his voice,\xe2\x80\x9d Jorgensen told me. \xe2\x80\x9cBut I could tell something was wrong. I asked what it was.\xe2\x80\x9d Then another voice said, \xe2\x80\x9cI have your son and I\xe2\x80\x99m going to shoot him in the head.\xe2\x80\x9d Jorgensen panicked and she hung up. Ten minutes later, she received confirmation from Tate\xe2\x80\x99s school that her son was safe, and now sitting in the principal\xe2\x80\x99s office. Even DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s mother received a scam call. Months before DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s ordeal, someone had called her mother claiming to be DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s brother, and asking for money to pay a hospital bill related to a car accident. But DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s mother could sense that something was off. \xe2\x80\x9cShe\xe2\x80\x99s hard of hearing, but she\xe2\x80\x99s still sharp,\xe2\x80\x9d DeStefano said. \xe2\x80\x9cShe hung up.\xe2\x80\x9d

Robin and Steve, in Brooklyn, eventually got their money back from Venmo. Today, they\xe2\x80\x99re able to joke about some aspects of the ordeal: the pizza-emoji instruction, for example. \xe2\x80\x9cBut we told everyone we knew to be aware of this very sophisticated thing,\xe2\x80\x9d Robin said. The family has created a plan for the next time. \xe2\x80\x9cIt doesn\xe2\x80\x99t seem like this scam is going to stop anytime soon,\xe2\x80\x9d Robin told me. \xe2\x80\x9cSo we came up with an extended-family password. If one of us is in trouble, others can verify that it\xe2\x80\x99s really them.\xe2\x80\x9d When I recently called up Mona, her mother-in-law, though, she confessed that she\xe2\x80\x99d already forgotten the family password: \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m going to have to go over it.\xe2\x80\x9d She added that it took her a while to accept one aspect of the call. \xe2\x80\x9cSeven hundred and fifty dollars,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cI still can\xe2\x80\x99t believe that\xe2\x80\x99s all I was worth.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x99\xa6

Search Annals of Artificial Intelligence A Brooklyn couple got a call from relatives who were being held ransom. Their voices\xe2\x80\x94 like many others these days\xe2\x80\x94 had been cloned. March 7, 2024 Illustration by Ariel Davis On a recent night, a woman named Robin was asleep next to her husband, Steve, in their Brooklyn home, when her phone buzzed on the bedside table. Robin is in her mid- thirties with long, dirty- blond hair. She works as an interior designer, specializing in luxury homes. The couple had gone out to a natural- wine bar in Cobble Hill that evening, and had come home a few hours earlier and gone to bed. Their two young children were asleep in bedrooms down the hall. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m always, like, kind of one ear awake,\xe2\x80\x9d Robin told me, recently. When her phone rang, she opened her eyes and looked at the caller I.D. It was her mother- in- law, Mona, who never called after midnight. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m, like, maybe it\xe2\x80\x99s a butt- dial,\xe2\x80\x9d Robin said. \xe2\x80\x9cSo I ignore it, and I try to roll over and go back to bed. But then I see it pop up again.\xe2\x80\x9d She picked up the phone, and, on the other end, she heard Mona\xe2\x80\x99s voice wailing and repeating the words \xe2\x80\x9cI can\xe2\x80\x99t do it, I can\xe2\x80\x99t do it.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cI thought she was trying to tell me that some horrible tragic thing had happened,\xe2\x80\x9d Robin told me. Mona and her husband, Bob, are in their seventies. She\xe2\x80\x99s a retired party planner, and he\xe2\x80\x99s a dentist. They spend the warm months in Bethesda, Maryland, and winters in Boca Raton, where they play pickleball and canasta. Robin\xe2\x80\x99s first thought was that there had been an accident. Robin\xe2\x80\x99s parents also winter in Florida, and she pictured the four of them in a car wreck. \xe2\x80\x9cYour brain does weird things in the middle of the night,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. Robin then heard what sounded like Bob\xe2\x80\x99s voice on the phone. (The family members requested that their names be changed to protect their privacy.) \xe2\x80\x9cMona, pass me the phone,\xe2\x80\x9d Bob\xe2\x80\x99s voice said, then, \xe2\x80\x9cGet Steve. Get Steve.\xe2\x80\x9d Robin took this\xe2\x80\x94 that they didn\xe2\x80\x99t want to tell her while she was alone\xe2\x80\x94 as another sign of their seriousness. She shook Steve awake. \xe2\x80\x9cI think it\xe2\x80\x99s your mom,\xe2\x80\x9d she told him. \xe2\x80\x9cI think she\xe2\x80\x99s telling me something terrible happened.\xe2\x80\x9d Steve, who has close- cropped hair and an athletic build, works in law enforcement. When he opened his eyes, he found Robin in a state of panic. \xe2\x80\x9cShe was screaming,\xe2\x80\x9d he recalled. \xe2\x80\x9cI thought her whole family was dead.\xe2\x80\x9d When he took the phone, he heard a relaxed male voice\xe2\x80\x94 possibly Southern\xe2\x80\x94 on the other end of the line. \xe2\x80\x9cYou\xe2\x80\x99re not gonna call the police,\xe2\x80\x9d the man said. \xe2\x80\x9cYou\xe2\x80\x99re not gonna tell anybody. I\xe2\x80\x99ve got a gun to your mom\xe2\x80\x99s head, and I\xe2\x80\x99m gonna blow her brains out if you don\xe2\x80\x99t do exactly what I say.\xe2\x80\x9d Steve used his own phone to call a colleague with experience in hostage negotiations. The colleague was muted, so that he could hear the call but wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t be heard. \xe2\x80\x9cYou hear this???\xe2\x80\x9d Steve texted him. \xe2\x80\x9cWhat should I do?\xe2\x80\x9d The colleague wrote back, \xe2\x80\x9cTaking notes. Keep talking.\xe2\x80\x9d The idea, Steve said, was to continue the conversation, delaying violence and trying to learn any useful information. \xe2\x80\x9cI want to hear her voice,\xe2\x80\x9d Steve said to the man on the phone. The man refused. \xe2\x80\x9cIf you ask me that again, I\xe2\x80\x99m gonna kill her,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cAre you fucking crazy?\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cO.K.,\xe2\x80\x9d Steve said. \xe2\x80\x9cWhat do you want?\xe2\x80\x9d The man demanded money for travel; he wanted five hundred dollars, sent through Venmo. \xe2\x80\x9cIt was such an insanely small amount of money for a human being,\xe2\x80\x9d Steve recalled. \xe2\x80\x9cBut also: I\xe2\x80\x99m obviously gonna pay this.\xe2\x80\x9d Robin, listening in, reasoned that someone had broken into Steve\xe2\x80\x99s parents\xe2\x80\x99 home to hold them up for a little cash. On the phone, the man gave Steve a Venmo account to send the money to. It didn\xe2\x80\x99t work, so he tried a few more, and eventually found one that did. The app asked what the transaction was for. \xe2\x80\x9cPut in a pizza emoji,\xe2\x80\x9d the man said. After Steve sent the five hundred dollars, the man patched in a female voice\xe2\x80\x94 a girlfriend, it seemed\xe2\x80\x94 who said that the money had come through, but that it wasn\xe2\x80\x99t enough. Steve asked if his mother would be released, and the man got upset that he was bringing this up with the woman listening. \xe2\x80\x9cWhoa, whoa, whoa,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cBaby, I\xe2\x80\x99ll call you later.\xe2\x80\x9d The implication, to Steve, was that the woman didn\xe2\x80\x99t know about the hostage situation. \xe2\x80\x9cThat made it even more real,\xe2\x80\x9d Steve told me. The man then asked for an additional two hundred and fifty dollars to get a ticket for his girlfriend. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve gotta get my baby mama down here to me,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. Steve sent the additional sum, and, when it processed, the man hung up. By this time, about twenty- five minutes had elapsed. Robin cried and Steve spoke to his colleague. \xe2\x80\x9cYou guys did great,\xe2\x80\x9d the colleague said. He told them to call Bob, since Mona\xe2\x80\x99s phone was clearly compromised, to make sure that he and Mona were now safe. After a few tries, Bob picked up the phone and handed it to Mona. \xe2\x80\x9cAre you at home?\xe2\x80\x9d Steve and Robin asked her. \xe2\x80\x9cAre you O.K.?\xe2\x80\x9d Mona sounded fine, but she was unsure of what they were talking about. \xe2\x80\x9cYeah, I\xe2\x80\x99m in bed,\xe2\x80\x9d she replied. \xe2\x80\x9cWhy?\xe2\x80\x9d Artificial intelligence is revolutionizing seemingly every aspect of our lives: medical diagnosis, weather forecasting, space exploration, and even mundane tasks like writing e- mails and searching the Internet. But with increased efficiencies and computational accuracy has come a Pandora\xe2\x80\x99s box of trouble. Deepfake video content is proliferating across the Internet. The month after Russia invaded Ukraine, a video surfaced on social media in which Ukraine\xe2\x80\x99s President, Volodymyr Zelensky, appeared to tell his troops to surrender. (He had not done so.) In early February of this year, Hong Kong police announced that a finance worker had been tricked into paying out twenty- five million dollars after taking part in a video conference with who he thought were members of his firm\xe2\x80\x99s senior staff. (They were not.) Thanks to large language models like ChatGPT, phishing e- mails have grown increasingly sophisticated, too. Steve and Robin, meanwhile, fell victim to another new scam, which uses A.I. to replicate a loved one\xe2\x80\x99s voice. \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99ve now passed through the uncanny valley,\xe2\x80\x9d Hany Farid, who studies generative A.I. and manipulated media at the University of California, Berkeley, told me. \xe2\x80\x9cI can now clone the voice of just about anybody and get them to say just about anything. And what you think would happen is exactly what\xe2\x80\x99s happening.\xe2\x80\x9d Robots aping human voices are not new, of course. In 1984, an Apple computer became one of the first that could read a text file in a tinny robotic voice of its own. \xe2\x80\x9cHello, I\xe2\x80\x99m Macintosh,\xe2\x80\x9d a squat machine announced to a live audience, at an unveiling with Steve Jobs. \xe2\x80\x9cIt sure is great to get out of that bag.\xe2\x80\x9d The computer took potshots at Apple\xe2\x80\x99s main competitor at the time, saying, \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99d like to share with you a maxim I thought of the first time I met an I.B.M. mainframe: never trust a computer you can\xe2\x80\x99t lift.\xe2\x80\x9d In 2011, Apple released Siri; inspired by \xe2\x80\x9cStar Trek\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x99s talking computers, the program could interpret precise commands\xe2\x80\x94 \xe2\x80\x9cPlay Steely Dan,\xe2\x80\x9d say, or, \xe2\x80\x9cCall Mom\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\x94 and respond with a limited vocabulary. Three years later, Amazon released Alexa. Synthesized voices were cohabiting with us. Still, until a few years ago, advances in synthetic voices had plateaued. They weren\xe2\x80\x99t entirely convincing. \xe2\x80\x9cIf I\xe2\x80\x99m trying to create a better version of Siri or G.P.S., what I care about is naturalness,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid explained. \xe2\x80\x9cDoes this sound like a human being and not like this creepy half- human, half- robot thing?\xe2\x80\x9d Replicating a specific voice is even harder. \xe2\x80\x9cNot only do I have to sound human ,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid went on. \xe2\x80\x9cI have to sound like you .\xe2\x80\x9d In recent years, though, the problem began to benefit from more money, more data\xe2\x80\x94 importantly, troves of voice recordings online\xe2\x80\x94 and breakthroughs in the underlying software used for generating speech. In 2019, this bore fruit: a Toronto- based A.I. company called Dessa cloned the podcaster Joe Rogan\xe2\x80\x99s voice. (Rogan responded with \xe2\x80\x9cawe\xe2\x80\x9d and acceptance on Instagram, at the time, adding, \xe2\x80\x9cThe future is gonna be really fucking weird, kids.\xe2\x80\x9d) But Dessa needed a lot of money and hundreds of hours of Rogan\xe2\x80\x99s very available voice to make their product. Their success was a one- off. In 2022, though, a New York- based company called ElevenLabs unveiled a service that produced impressive clones of virtually any voice quickly; breathing sounds had been incorporated, and more than two dozen languages could be cloned. ElevenLabs\xe2\x80\x99s technology is now widely available. \xe2\x80\x9cYou can just navigate to an app, pay five dollars a month, feed it forty- five seconds of someone\xe2\x80\x99s voice, and then clone that voice,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid told me. The company is now valued at more than a billion dollars, and the rest of Big Tech is chasing closely behind. The designers of Microsoft\xe2\x80\x99s Vall- E cloning program, which d\xc3\xa9buted last year, used sixty thousand hours of English- language audiobook narration from more than seven thousand speakers. Vall- E, which is not available to the public, can reportedly replicate the voice and \xe2\x80\x9cacoustic environment\xe2\x80\x9d of a speaker with just a three- second sample. Voice- cloning technology has undoubtedly improved some lives. The Voice Keeper is among a handful of companies that are now \xe2\x80\x9cbanking\xe2\x80\x9d the voices of those suffering from voice- depriving diseases like A.L.S., Parkinson\xe2\x80\x99s, and throat cancer, so that, later, they can continue speaking with their own voice through text- to- speech software. A South Korean company recently launched what it describes as the first \xe2\x80\x9cAI memorial service,\xe2\x80\x9d which allows people to \xe2\x80\x9clive in the cloud\xe2\x80\x9d after their deaths and \xe2\x80\x9cspeak\xe2\x80\x9d to future generations. The company suggests that this can \xe2\x80\x9calleviate the pain of the death of your loved ones.\xe2\x80\x9d The technology has other legal, if less altruistic, applications. Celebrities can use voice- cloning programs to \xe2\x80\x9cloan\xe2\x80\x9d their voices to record advertisements and other content: the College Football Hall of Famer Keith Byars, for example, recently let a chicken chain in Ohio use a clone of his voice to take orders. The film industry has also benefitted. Actors in films can now \xe2\x80\x9cspeak\xe2\x80\x9d other languages\xe2\x80\x94 English, say, when a foreign movie is released in the U.S. \xe2\x80\x9cThat means no more subtitles, and no more dubbing,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid said. \xe2\x80\x9cEverybody can speak whatever language you want.\xe2\x80\x9d Multiple publications, including The New Yorker , use ElevenLabs to offer audio narrations of stories. Last year, New York\xe2\x80\x99s mayor, Eric Adams, sent out A.I.- enabled robocalls in Mandarin and Yiddish\xe2\x80\x94 languages he does not speak. (Privacy advocates called this a \xe2\x80\x9ccreepy vanity project.\xe2\x80\x9d) But, more often, the technology seems to be used for nefarious purposes, like fraud. This has become easier now that TikTok, YouTube, and Instagram store endless videos of regular people talking. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s simple,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid explained. \xe2\x80\x9cYou take thirty or sixty seconds of a kid\xe2\x80\x99s voice and log in to ElevenLabs, and pretty soon Grandma\xe2\x80\x99s getting a call in Grandson\xe2\x80\x99s voice saying, \xe2\x80\x98Grandma, I\xe2\x80\x99m in trouble, I\xe2\x80\x99ve been in an accident.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d A financial request is almost always the end game. Farid went on, \xe2\x80\x9cAnd here\xe2\x80\x99s the thing: the bad guy can fail ninety- nine per cent of the time, and they will still become very, very rich. It\xe2\x80\x99s a numbers game.\xe2\x80\x9d The prevalence of these illegal efforts is difficult to measure, but, anecdotally, they\xe2\x80\x99ve been on the rise for a few years. In 2020, a corporate attorney in Philadelphia took a call from what he thought was his son, who said he had been injured in a car wreck involving a pregnant woman and needed nine thousand dollars to post bail. (He found out it was a scam when his daughter- in- law called his son\xe2\x80\x99s office, where he was safely at work.) In January, voters in New Hampshire received a robocall call from Joe Biden\xe2\x80\x99s voice telling them not to vote in the primary. (The man who admitted to generating the call said that he had used ElevenLabs software.) \xe2\x80\x9cI didn\xe2\x80\x99t think about it at the time that it wasn\xe2\x80\x99t his real voice,\xe2\x80\x9d an elderly Democrat in New Hampshire told the Associated Press. \xe2\x80\x9cThat\xe2\x80\x99s how convincing it was.\xe2\x80\x9d Predictably, technology has outstripped regulation. Current copyright laws don\xe2\x80\x99t protect a person\xe2\x80\x99s voice. \xe2\x80\x9cA key question is whether authentication tools can keep up with advances in deepfake synthesis,\xe2\x80\x9d Senator Jon Ossoff, of Georgia, who chaired a Senate Judiciary Committee hearing on the matter last year, told me. \xe2\x80\x9cCan we get good enough fast enough at discerning real from fake, or will we lose the ability to verify the authenticity of voices, images, video, and other media?\xe2\x80\x9d He described the matter as an \xe2\x80\x9curgent\xe2\x80\x9d one for lawmakers. In January, a bipartisan group introduced the QUIET Act, which would increase penalties for those who use A.I. to impersonate people. In Arizona, a state senator introduced a bill that would designate A.I. as a weapon when used in conjunction with a crime, also allowing lengthier sentences. The Federal Trade Commission, which investigates consumer fraud, reported that Americans lost more than two million dollars to impostor scams of various kinds in 2022. Last year, the F.T.C. put out a voice- cloning advisory, noting, \xe2\x80\x9cIf the caller says to wire money, send cryptocurrency, or buy gift cards and give them the card numbers and PINs, those could be signs of a scam.\xe2\x80\x9d But it, too, has not yet created any guidelines for the use of voice- cloning technology. Even if laws are enacted, policing them will be exceedingly difficult. Scammers can use encrypted apps to execute their schemes, and calls are completed in minutes. \xe2\x80\x9cBy the time ller I.D. read \xe2\x80\x9cunknown,\xe2\x80\x9d so DeStefano ignored it at first. Then she reconsidered: Brianna, her older daughter, was on a ski trip up north, and, DeStefano thought, maybe something had happened. She took the call on speaker phone. \xe2\x80\x9cMom, I messed up!\xe2\x80\x9d Briana\xe2\x80\x99s voice said, sobbing in her uniquely controlled way. A man with a Spanish accent could be heard telling her, \xe2\x80\x9cLay down and put your head back.\xe2\x80\x9d Then Briana said, \xe2\x80\x9cMom, these bad men have me. Help me, help me, help me.\xe2\x80\x9d One of the men took the phone, as Briana sobbed and pleaded in the background. \xe2\x80\x9cI have your daughter,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cIf you seek any help from anyone, I\xe2\x80\x99ll pump her stomach so full of drugs.\xe2\x80\x9d He\xe2\x80\x99d have his way with her, he continued, and then he\xe2\x80\x99d leave her for dead. DeStefano ran into the dance studio and screamed for help. Three other mothers responded: one called 911, one called DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s husband, and one sat with DeStefano while she talked on the phone. First, the man demanded a million dollars, but DeStefano said that wasn\xe2\x80\x99t possible, so he lowered the sum to fifty thousand. As they discussed how to get the money to him, the mother who\xe2\x80\x99d called 911 came back inside and said that she\xe2\x80\x99d learned that the call might be a scam. DeStefano, who considers herself \xe2\x80\x9cpretty savvy,\xe2\x80\x9d was unconvinced. \xe2\x80\x9cI talked to her ,\xe2\x80\x9d DeStefano replied. She continued speaking to the man, who decided that he wanted to arrange a physical pickup of the money: a white van would meet DeStefano somewhere, and someone would put a bag over her head, and bring her to him. She recalled, \xe2\x80\x9cHe said I had better have all the cash, or else we were both dead.\xe2\x80\x9d Soon, though, the second mother hurried over. She had located DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s husband, who confirmed that he was with Briana. DeStefano eventually got ahold of her older daughter. \xe2\x80\x9cI have no idea what\xe2\x80\x99s going on, or what you\xe2\x80\x99re talking about,\xe2\x80\x9d Briana told her. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m with Dad.\xe2\x80\x9d Eventually, DeStefano returned to her phone call. \xe2\x80\x9cI called the guys out for being the lowest of the low,\xe2\x80\x9d DeStefano said. \xe2\x80\x9cI used vulgar words. Then I just hung up.\xe2\x80\x9d DeStefano went public with her experience, eventually testifying about it before the Senate Judiciary Committee. Other victims reached out. Another mother at the dance studio had a cousin who\xe2\x80\x99d been scammed just two weeks earlier. \xe2\x80\x9cThe call came in from her daughter\xe2\x80\x99s phone, and she actually sent fifteen hundred dollars,\xe2\x80\x9d DeStefano said. She told me that a friend had received a call from what sounded like her nine- year- old son: \xe2\x80\x9cHe\xe2\x80\x99d been kidnapped, he said. But she\xe2\x80\x99d just tucked him in bed after reading a story, so she knew it wasn\xe2\x80\x99t true.\xe2\x80\x9d RaeLee Jorgensen, a thirty- four- year- old teacher\xe2\x80\x99s aide, contacted DeStefano. Last April, while waiting for her two youngest children to get out of school, she received a phone call from her oldest son\xe2\x80\x99s number. \xe2\x80\x9cHey, Mom,\xe2\x80\x9d her fourteen- year- old son\xe2\x80\x99s voice said. \xe2\x80\x9cThis is Tate.\xe2\x80\x9d He was using his family nickname. \xe2\x80\x9cAnd it was his voice,\xe2\x80\x9d Jorgensen told me. \xe2\x80\x9cBut I could tell something was wrong. I asked what it was.\xe2\x80\x9d Then another voice said, \xe2\x80\x9cI have your son and I\xe2\x80\x99m going to shoot him in the head.\xe2\x80\x9d Jorgensen panicked and she hung up. Ten minutes later, she received confirmation from Tate\xe2\x80\x99s school that her son was safe, and now sitting in the principal\xe2\x80\x99s office. Even DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s mother received a scam call. Months before DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s ordeal, someone had called her mother claiming to be DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s brother, and asking for money to pay a hospital bill related to a car accident. But DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s mother could sense that something was off. \xe2\x80\x9cShe\xe2\x80\x99s hard of hearing, but she\xe2\x80\x99s still sharp,\xe2\x80\x9d DeStefano said. \xe2\x80\x9cShe hung up.\xe2\x80\x9d Robin and Steve, in Brooklyn, eventually got their money back from Venmo. Today, they\xe2\x80\x99re able to joke about some aspects of the ordeal: the pizza- emoji instruction, for example. \xe2\x80\x9cBut we told everyone we knew to be aware of this very sophisticated thing,\xe2\x80\x9d Robin said. The family has created a plan for the next time. \xe2\x80\x9cIt doesn\xe2\x80\x99t seem like this scam is going to stop anytime soon,\xe2\x80\x9d Robin told me. \xe2\x80\x9cSo we came up with an extended- family password. If one of us is in trouble, others can verify that it\xe2\x80\x99s really them.\xe2\x80\x9d When I recently called up Mona, her mother- in- law, though, she confessed that she\xe2\x80\x99d already forgotten the family password: \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m going to have to go over it.\xe2\x80\x9d She added that it took her a while to accept one aspect of the call. \xe2\x80\x9cSeven hundred and fifty dollars,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cI still can\xe2\x80\x99t believe that\xe2\x80\x99s all I was worth.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x99\xa6 Election 2024: Full Coverage The New Yorker endorses Kamala Harris . Donald Trump\xe2\x80\x99s final days on the campaign trail . The election season that fell out of a coconut tree . How alarmed should we be if Trump wins again ? From 2017: Why facts don\xe2\x80\x99t change our minds . Sign up for our daily newsletter to receive the best stories from The New Yorker . Charles Bethea , a staff writer, has contributed to The New Yorker since 2008. He covers crime, politics, food, local media, and the A He told them to call Bob, since Mona\xe2\x80\x99s phone was clearly compromised, to make sure that he and Mona were now safe. After a few tries, Bob picked up the phone and handed it to Mona. \xe2\x80\x9cAre you at home?\xe2\x80\x9d Steve and Robin asked her. \xe2\x80\x9cAre you O.K.?\xe2\x80\x9d Mona sounded fine, but she was unsure of what they were talking about. \xe2\x80\x9cYeah, I\xe2\x80\x99m in bed,\xe2\x80\x9d she replied. \xe2\x80\x9cWhy?\xe2\x80\x9d Artificial intelligence is revolutionizing seemingly every aspect of our lives: medical diagnosis, weather forecasting, space exploration, and even mundane tasks like writing e- mails and searching the Internet. But with increased efficiencies and computational accuracy has come a Pandora\xe2\x80\x99s box of trouble. Deepfake video content is proliferating across the Internet. The month after Russia invaded Ukraine, a video surfaced on social media in which Ukraine\xe2\x80\x99s President, Volodymyr Zelensky, appeared to tell his troops to surrender. (He had not done so.) In early February of this year, Hong Kong police announced that a finance worker had been tricked into paying out twenty- five million dollars after taking part in a video conference with who he thought were members of his firm\xe2\x80\x99s senior staff. (They were not.) Thanks to large language models like ChatGPT, phishing e- mails have grown increasingly sophisticated, too. Steve and Robin, meanwhile, fell victim to another new scam, which uses A.I. to replicate a loved one\xe2\x80\x99s voice. \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99ve now passed through the uncanny valley,\xe2\x80\x9d Hany Farid, who studies generative A.I. and manipulated media at the University of California, Berkeley, told me. \xe2\x80\x9cI can now clone the voice of just about anybody and get them to say just about anything. And what you think would happen is exactly what\xe2\x80\x99s happening.\xe2\x80\x9d Robots aping human voices are not new, of course. In 1984, an Apple computer became one of the first that could read a text file in a tinny robotic voice of its own. \xe2\x80\x9cHello, I\xe2\x80\x99m Macintosh,\xe2\x80\x9d a squat machine announced to a live audience, at an unveiling with Steve Jobs. \xe2\x80\x9cIt sure is great to get out of that bag.\xe2\x80\x9d The computer took potshots at Apple\xe2\x80\x99s main competitor at the time, saying, \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99d like to share with you a maxim I thought of the first time I met an I.B.M. mainframe: never trust a computer you can\xe2\x80\x99t lift.\xe2\x80\x9d In 2011, Apple released Siri; inspired by \xe2\x80\x9cStar Trek\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x99s talking computers, the program could interpret precise commands\xe2\x80\x94 \xe2\x80\x9cPlay Steely Dan,\xe2\x80\x9d say, or, \xe2\x80\x9cCall Mom\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\x94 and respond with a limited vocabulary. Three years later, Amazon released Alexa. Synthesized voices were cohabiting with us. Still, until a few years ago, advances in synthetic voices had plateaued. They weren\xe2\x80\x99t entirely convincing. \xe2\x80\x9cIf I\xe2\x80\x99m trying to create a better version of Siri or G.P.S., what I care about is naturalness,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid explained. \xe2\x80\x9cDoes this sound like a human being and not like this creepy half- human, half- robot thing?\xe2\x80\x9d Replicating a specific voice is even harder. \xe2\x80\x9cNot only do I have to sound human ,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid went on. \xe2\x80\x9cI have to sound like you .\xe2\x80\x9d In recent years, though, the problem began to benefit from more money, more data\xe2\x80\x94 importantly, troves of voice recordings online\xe2\x80\x94 and breakthroughs in the underlying software used for generating speech. In 2019, this bore fruit: a Toronto- based A.I. company called Dessa cloned the podcaster Joe Rogan\xe2\x80\x99s voice. (Rogan responded with \xe2\x80\x9cawe\xe2\x80\x9d and acceptance on Instagram, at the time, adding, \xe2\x80\x9cThe future is gonna be really fucking weird, kids.\xe2\x80\x9d) But Dessa needed a lot of money and hundreds of hours of Rogan\xe2\x80\x99s very available voice to make their product. Their success was a one- off. In 2022, though, a New York- based company called ElevenLabs unveiled a service that produced impressive clones of virtually any voice quickly; breathing sounds had been incorporated, and more than two dozen languages could be cloned. ElevenLabs\xe2\x80\x99s technology is now widely available. \xe2\x80\x9cYou can just navigate to an app, pay five dollars a month, feed it forty- five seconds of someone\xe2\x80\x99s voice, and then clone that voice,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid told me. The company is now valued at more than a billion dollars, and the rest of Big Tech is chasing closely behind. The designers of Microsoft\xe2\x80\x99s Vall- E cloning program, which d\xc3\xa9buted last year, used sixty thousand hours of English- language audiobook narration from more than seven thousand speakers. Vall- E, which is not available to the public, can reportedly replicate the voice and \xe2\x80\x9cacoustic environment\xe2\x80\x9d of a speaker with just a three- second sample. Voice- cloning technology has undoubtedly improved some lives. The Voice Keeper is among a handful of companies that are now \xe2\x80\x9cbanking\xe2\x80\x9d the voices of those suffering from voice- depriving diseases like A.L.S., Parkinson\xe2\x80\x99s, and throat cancer, so that, later, they can continue speaking with their own voice through text- to- speech software. A South Korean company recently launched what it describes as the first \xe2\x80\x9cAI memorial service,\xe2\x80\x9d which allows people to \xe2\x80\x9clive in the cloud\xe2\x80\x9d after their deaths and \xe2\x80\x9cspeak\xe2\x80\x9d to future generations. The company suggests that this can \xe2\x80\x9calleviate the pain of the death of your loved ones.\xe2\x80\x9d The technology has other legal, if less altruistic, applications. Celebrities can use voice- cloning programs to \xe2\x80\x9cloan\xe2\x80\x9d their voices to record advertisements and other content: the College Football Hall of Famer Keith Byars, for example, recently let a chicken chain in Ohio use a clone of his voice to take orders. The film industry has also benefitted. Actors in films can now \xe2\x80\x9cspeak\xe2\x80\x9d other languages\xe2\x80\x94 English, say, when a foreign movie is released in the U.S. \xe2\x80\x9cThat means no more subtitles, and no more dubbing,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid said. \xe2\x80\x9cEverybody can speak whatever language you want.\xe2\x80\x9d Multiple publications, including The New Yorker , use ElevenLabs to offer audio narrations of stories. Last year, New York\xe2\x80\x99s mayor, Eric Adams, sent out A.I.- enabled robocalls in Mandarin and Yiddish\xe2\x80\x94 languages he does not speak. (Privacy advocates called this a \xe2\x80\x9ccreepy vanity project.\xe2\x80\x9d) But, more often, the technology seems to be used for nefarious purposes, like fraud. This has become easier now that TikTok, YouTube, and Instagram store endless videos of regular people talking. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s simple,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid explained. \xe2\x80\x9cYou take thirty or sixty seconds of a kid\xe2\x80\x99s voice and log in to ElevenLabs, and pretty soon Grandma\xe2\x80\x99s getting a call in Grandson\xe2\x80\x99s voice saying, \xe2\x80\x98Grandma, I\xe2\x80\x99m in trouble, I\xe2\x80\x99ve been in an accident.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d A financial request is almost always the end game. Farid went on, \xe2\x80\x9cAnd here\xe2\x80\x99s the thing: the bad guy can fail ninety- nine per cent of the time, and they will still become very, very rich. It\xe2\x80\x99s a numbers game.\xe2\x80\x9d The prevalence of these illegal efforts is difficult to measure, but, anecdotally, they\xe2\x80\x99ve been on the rise for a few years. In 2020, a corporate attorney in Philadelphia took a call from what he thought was his son, who said he had been injured in a car wreck involving a pregnant woman and needed nine thousand dollars to post bail. (He found out it was a scam when his daughter- in- law called his son\xe2\x80\x99s office, where he was safely at work.) In January, voters in New Hampshire received a robocall call from Joe Biden\xe2\x80\x99s voice telling them not to vote in the primary. (The man who admitted to generating the call said that he had used ElevenLabs software.) \xe2\x80\x9cI didn\xe2\x80\x99t think about it at the time that it wasn\xe2\x80\x99t his real voice,\xe2\x80\x9d an elderly Democrat in New Hampshire told the Associated Press. \xe2\x80\x9cThat\xe2\x80\x99s how convincing it was.\xe2\x80\x9d Predictably, technology has outstripped regulation. Current copyright laws don\xe2\x80\x99t protect a person\xe2\x80\x99s voice. \xe2\x80\x9cA key question is whether authentication tools can keep up with advances in deepfake synthesis,\xe2\x80\x9d Senator Jon Ossoff, of Georgia, who chaired a Senate Judiciary Committee hearing on the matter last year, told me. \xe2\x80\x9cCan we get good enough fast enough at discerning real from fake, or will we lose the ability to verify the authenticity of voices, images, video, and other media?\xe2\x80\x9d He described the matter as an \xe2\x80\x9curgent\xe2\x80\x9d one for lawmakers. In January, a bipartisan group introduced the QUIET Act, which would increase penalties for those who use A.I. to impersonate people. In Arizona, a state senator introduced a bill that would designate A.I. as a weapon when used in conjunction with a crime, also allowing lengthier sentences. The Federal Trade Commission, which investigates consumer fraud, reported that Americans lost more than two million dollars to impostor scams of various kinds in 2022. Last year, the F.T.C. put out a voice- cloning advisory, noting, \xe2\x80\x9cIf the caller says to wire money, send cryptocurrency, or buy gift cards and give them the card numbers and PINs, those could be signs of a scam.\xe2\x80\x9d But it, too, has not yet created any guidelines for the use of voice- cloning technology. Even if laws are enacted, policing them will be exceedingly difficult. Scammers can use encrypted apps to execute their schemes, and calls are completed in minutes. \xe2\x80\x9cBy the time you get there, the scam is over, and everybody\xe2\x80\x99s moved on,\xe2\x80\x9d Farid said. A decade ago, the F.T.C. sponsored a competition to counter the rise of robocalls, and one of its winners went on to create Nomorobo, a call- blocking service that has helped to reduce\xe2\x80\x94 but not eliminate\xe2\x80\x94 the phenomenon. Late last year, the commission offered a twenty- five- thousand- dollar prize for the development of new ways to protect consumers from voice cloning. It received around seventy- five submissions, which focus on prevention, authentication, and real- time detection. Some of the submissions use artificial intelligence, while others rely on metadata or watermarking. (Judging will be completed by April.) Will Maxson, who is managing the F.T.C.\xe2\x80\x99s challenge, told me, \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99re hoping we\xe2\x80\x99ll spur some innovators to come up with products and services that will help reduce this new threat.\xe2\x80\x9d But it\xe2\x80\x99s not at all clear how effective they will be. \xe2\x80\x9cThere are no silver bullets,\xe2\x80\x9d he acknowledged. A few months ago, Farid, the Berkeley professor, participated in a Zoom call with Barack Obama. The former President was interested, he said, in learning about generative A.I. During the Zoom call, Farid found himself in an increasingly familiar online state of mind: doubt. \xe2\x80\x9cPeople have spent so much time trying to make deepfakes with Obama that I spent, like, the first ten minutes being, like, I don\xe2\x80\x99t know, man, I don\xe2\x80\x99t think this is him,\xe2\x80\x9d he said, laughing. In the end, he determined that it was the real Obama. Still, the experience was unnerving. \xe2\x80\x9cShit\xe2\x80\x99s getting weird ,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. One Friday last January, Jennifer DeStefano, who lives in Scottsdale, Arizona, got a call while walking into a dance studio where the younger of her two teen- age daughters, Aubrey, had just wrapped up a rehearsal. The caller I.D. read \xe2\x80\x9cunknown,\xe2\x80\x9d so DeStefano ignored it at first. Then she reconsidered: Brianna, her older daughter, was on a ski trip up north, and, DeStefano thought, maybe something had happened. She took the call on speaker phone. \xe2\x80\x9cMom, I messed up!\xe2\x80\x9d Briana\xe2\x80\x99s voice said, sobbing in her uniquely controlled way. A man with a Spanish accent could be heard telling her, \xe2\x80\x9cLay down and put your head back.\xe2\x80\x9d Then Briana said, \xe2\x80\x9cMom, these bad men have me. Help me, help me, help me.\xe2\x80\x9d One of the men took the phone, as Briana sobbed and pleaded in the background. \xe2\x80\x9cI have your daughter,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cIf you seek any help from anyone, I\xe2\x80\x99ll pump her stomach so full of drugs.\xe2\x80\x9d He\xe2\x80\x99d have his way with her, he continued, and then he\xe2\x80\x99d leave her for dead. DeStefano ran into the dance studio and screamed for help. Three other mothers responded: one called 911, one called DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s husband, and one sat with DeStefano while she talked on the phone. First, the man demanded a million dollars, but DeStefano said that wasn\xe2\x80\x99t possible, so he lowered the sum to fifty thousand. As they discussed how to get the money to him, the mother who\xe2\x80\x99d called 911 came back inside and said that she\xe2\x80\x99d learned that the call might be a scam. DeStefano, who considers herself \xe2\x80\x9cpretty savvy,\xe2\x80\x9d was unconvinced. \xe2\x80\x9cI talked to her ,\xe2\x80\x9d DeStefano replied. She continued speaking to the man, who decided that he wanted to arrange a physical pickup of the money: a white van would meet DeStefano somewhere, and someone would put a bag over her head, and bring her to him. She recalled, \xe2\x80\x9cHe said I had better have all the cash, or else we were both dead.\xe2\x80\x9d Soon, though, the second mother hurried over. She had located DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s husband, who confirmed that he was with Briana. DeStefano eventually got ahold of her older daughter. \xe2\x80\x9cI have no idea what\xe2\x80\x99s going on, or what you\xe2\x80\x99re talking about,\xe2\x80\x9d Briana told her. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m with Dad.\xe2\x80\x9d Eventually, DeStefano returned to her phone call. \xe2\x80\x9cI called the guys out for being the lowest of the low,\xe2\x80\x9d DeStefano said. \xe2\x80\x9cI used vulgar words. Then I just hung up.\xe2\x80\x9d DeStefano went public with her experience, eventually testifying about it before the Senate Judiciary Committee. Other victims reached out. Another mother at the dance studio had a cousin who\xe2\x80\x99d been scammed just two weeks earlier. \xe2\x80\x9cThe call came in from her daughter\xe2\x80\x99s phone, and she actually sent fifteen hundred dollars,\xe2\x80\x9d DeStefano said. She told me that a friend had received a call from what sounded like her nine- year- old son: \xe2\x80\x9cHe\xe2\x80\x99d been kidnapped, he said. But she\xe2\x80\x99d just tucked him in bed after reading a story, so she knew it wasn\xe2\x80\x99t true.\xe2\x80\x9d RaeLee Jorgensen, a thirty- four- year- old teacher\xe2\x80\x99s aide, contacted DeStefano. Last April, while waiting for her two youngest children to get out of school, she received a phone call from her oldest son\xe2\x80\x99s number. \xe2\x80\x9cHey, Mom,\xe2\x80\x9d her fourteen- year- old son\xe2\x80\x99s voice said. \xe2\x80\x9cThis is Tate.\xe2\x80\x9d He was using his family nickname. \xe2\x80\x9cAnd it was his voice,\xe2\x80\x9d Jorgensen told me. \xe2\x80\x9cBut I could tell something was wrong. I asked what it was.\xe2\x80\x9d Then another voice said, \xe2\x80\x9cI have your son and I\xe2\x80\x99m going to shoot him in the head.\xe2\x80\x9d Jorgensen panicked and she hung up. Ten minutes later, she received confirmation from Tate\xe2\x80\x99s school that her son was safe, and now sitting in the principal\xe2\x80\x99s office. Even DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s mother received a scam call. Months before DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s ordeal, someone had called her mother claiming to be DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s brother, and asking for money to pay a hospital bill related to a car accident. But DeStefano\xe2\x80\x99s mother could sense that something was off. \xe2\x80\x9cShe\xe2\x80\x99s hard of hearing, but she\xe2\x80\x99s still sharp,\xe2\x80\x9d DeStefano said. \xe2\x80\x9cShe hung up.\xe2\x80\x9d Robin and Steve, in Brooklyn, eventually got their money back from Venmo. Today, they\xe2\x80\x99re able to joke about some aspects of the ordeal: the pizza- emoji instruction, for example. \xe2\x80\x9cBut we told everyone we knew to be aware of this very sophisticated thing,\xe2\x80\x9d Robin said. The family has created a plan for the next time. \xe2\x80\x9cIt doesn\xe2\x80\x99t seem like this scam is going to stop anytime soon,\xe2\x80\x9d Robin told me. \xe2\x80\x9cSo we came up with an extended- family password. If one of us is in trouble, others can verify that it\xe2\x80\x99s really them.\xe2\x80\x9d When I recently called up Mona, her mother- in- law, though, she confessed that she\xe2\x80\x99d already forgotten the family password: \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m going to have to go over it.\xe2\x80\x9d She added that it took her a while to accept one aspect of the call. \xe2\x80\x9cSeven hundred and fifty dollars,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cI still can\xe2\x80\x99t believe that\xe2\x80\x99s all I was worth.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x99\xa6 Election 2024: Full Coverage The New Yorker endorses Kamala Harris .
